tier_2_handbook.definition: Soft tissue sarcomas are a rare and heterogeneous group of solid tumors of mesenchymal origin, accounting for only 1% of all adult malignancies and 15% of childhood malignancies. They can be broadly divided into sarcomas of soft tissues (including fat, muscle, nerve and nerve sheath, blood vessels, and other connective tissues) and sarcomas of bone. The anatomic site of the primary disease represents an important variable that influences treatment and outcome. Extremities (43%), the trunk (10%), visceral (19%), retroperitoneum (15%), or head and neck (9%) are the most common primary sites. Tumors arising in the abdominal cavity more commonly metastasize to the liver and peritoneum, while most other soft tissue sarcomas metastasize to the lungs. The true incidence is underestimated, especially because gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST) may not have been included in registry databases before 2001. Prior radiation therapy (RT) is a known risk factor, as are various chemicals and genetic syndromes. More than 50 different histologic subtypes have been identified. Pathologic assessment is critical, and morphologic diagnosis based on microscopic examination remains the gold standard. However, ancillary techniques like immunohistochemistry, cytogenetics, and molecular genetic testing are essential for accurate diagnosis and classification, as many subtypes are defined by specific genetic alterations. The 2020 World Health Organization classification organizes these tumors into distinct categories based on line of differentiation (e.g., adipocytic, fibroblastic/myofibroblastic, smooth muscle, skeletal muscle, vascular, peripheral nerve sheath, tumors of uncertain differentiation). Staging uses the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) Cancer Staging Manual, Eighth Edition, which incorporates TNM classification, tumor size, histologic grade (using the FNCLCC system), and depth. Multidisciplinary evaluation and treatment by a team with sarcoma expertise is mandatory for optimal management.

subtypes.name: Adipocytic Tumors (Liposarcomas)
subtypes.description: Includes atypical lipomatous tumor/well-differentiated liposarcoma (ALT/WDLPS), dedifferentiated liposarcoma, myxoid/round cell liposarcoma, and pleomorphic liposarcoma. ALT/WDLPS are locally infiltrative but rarely metastasize; dedifferentiated liposarcomas are high-grade and aggressive. Myxoid/round cell liposarcoma has a unique propensity for extrapulmonary metastases, especially to the spine.
subtypes.frequency: Liposarcomas are among the most common STS subtypes. The exact frequency varies by anatomic site (e.g., ALT/WDLPS is common in the retroperitoneum).
subtypes.key_associations: ALT/WDLPS: amplification of region 12q13-15 including MDM2, CDK4, HMGA2. Myxoid/round cell liposarcoma: characteristic translocations t(12;16)(q13;p11) resulting in FUS::DDIT3 fusion or t(12;22)(q13;q12) resulting in EWSR1::DDIT3 fusion.

subtypes.name: Fibroblastic and Myofibroblastic Tumors
subtypes.description: Includes entities such as nodular fasciitis, myxofibrosarcoma, low-grade fibromyxoid sarcoma, sclerosing epithelioid fibrosarcoma, and the distinct entity of desmoid fibromatosis (aggressive fibromatosis).
subtypes.frequency: Frequency varies widely by subtype. Desmoid tumors are a significant subset.
subtypes.key_associations: Low-grade fibromyxoid sarcoma/sclerosing epithelioid fibrosarcoma: characteristic translocations t(7;16)(q33;p11) or t(11;16)(p11;p11) resulting in FUS::CREB3L2 or FUS::CREB3L1 fusions. Desmoid fibromatosis: CTNNB1 or APC mutations.

subtypes.name: So-called Fibrohistiocytic Tumors
subtypes.description: This category includes benign and borderline entities. The most relevant sarcoma is tenosynovial giant cell tumor (TGCT)/pigmented villonodular synovitis (PVNS).
subtypes.frequency: TGCT/PVNS is a relatively common tumor of the synovial joints.

subtypes.name: Vascular Tumors
subtypes.description: Includes epithelioid hemangioendothelioma (EHE) and angiosarcoma. Angiosarcoma is an aggressive tumor of endothelial differentiation.
subtypes.frequency: Angiosarcoma is a rare but aggressive subtype.
subtypes.key_associations: EHE: Characterized by t(1;3)(p36;q25) leading to WWTR1::CAMTA1 fusion or t(X;11)(p11.23;q22.1) leading to YAP1::TFE3 fusion.

subtypes.name: Smooth Muscle Tumors
subtypes.description: Includes leiomyoma and leiomyosarcoma (LMS). LMS is a common sarcoma subtype, often arising in the retroperitoneum or uterus.
subtypes.frequency: LMS is one of the more common STS subtypes.
subtypes.key_associations: LMS typically has complex genetic alterations without a single defining translocation.

subtypes.name: Skeletal Muscle Tumors
subtypes.description: Includes rhabdomyoma and rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS). RMS is the most common STS of children and adolescents. The main histologic subtypes are embryonal, alveolar, and pleomorphic.
subtypes.frequency: RMS accounts for 2-5% of all adult STS but is the most common childhood STS.
subtypes.key_associations: Alveolar RMS: Characteristic translocations t(2;13)(q35;q14) or t(1;13)(p36;q14) resulting in PAX3::FOXO1 or PAX7::FOXO1 fusions. Embryonal RMS: Complex alterations involving MYOD1, KRAS, TP53, NF1, etc.

subtypes.name: Peripheral Nerve Sheath Tumors
subtypes.description: Includes malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor (MPNST).
subtypes.frequency: MPNST is a rare but aggressive sarcoma, often arising in the setting of neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1).
subtypes.key_associations: MPNST: Associated with NF1, CDKN2A, and EED or SUZ12 alterations.

subtypes.name: Tumors of Uncertain Differentiation
subtypes.description: A large and heterogeneous group including synovial sarcoma, epithelioid sarcoma, alveolar soft part sarcoma (ASPS), clear cell sarcoma, extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma, desmoplastic small round cell tumor, PEComa, and undifferentiated sarcoma.
subtypes.frequency: Synovial sarcoma is a relatively common subtype in young adults.
subtypes.key_associations: Synovial sarcoma: Characteristic translocation t(X;18)(p11;q11) resulting in SS18::SSX1, SS18::SSX2, or SS18::SSX4 fusion. ASPS: Characteristic der(17)t(X;17)(p11;q25) resulting in ASPL::TFE3 fusion. Clear cell sarcoma: Characterized by EWSR1::ATF1 or EWSR1::CREB1 fusions. Epithelioid sarcoma: Characterized by INI1 (SMARCB-1) loss.

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The molecular pathogenesis of soft tissue sarcomas is highly diverse and often defines specific histologic entities. Sarcomas can be broadly divided into two major genetic groups: 1) sarcomas with specific genetic alterations (e.g., chromosomal translocations or point mutations) and usually simple karyotypes; and 2) sarcomas with non-specific genetic alterations and complex unbalanced karyotypes. For translocation sarcomas, the fusion genes resulting from chromosomal translocations provide crucial diagnostic and prognostic information. For example, in alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma, the PAX7-FOXO1 fusion is associated with a more favorable prognosis compared to PAX3-FOXO1. In synovial sarcoma, the SS18-SSX fusion type (SS18-SSX1 vs. SS18-SSX2) may have prognostic implications, though data is conflicting. In myxoid/round cell liposarcoma, the fusion transcript type (FUS-DDIT3 vs. EWSR1-DDIT3) does not appear to affect outcome. Molecular diagnostic techniques used include conventional cytogenetics, fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH), polymerase chain reaction (PCR), and next-generation sequencing (NGS). NGS may be beneficial for identifying actionable mutations, selecting patients for immunotherapy (e.g., based on TMB-H or MSI-H status), and enrolling patients in clinical trials. Key recurrent genetic aberrations are listed in the guidelines' table (SARC-C). Some notable examples include: Alveolar RMS: PAX3::FOXO1 or PAX7::FOXO1. Synovial sarcoma: SS18::SSX1 or SS18::SSX2. Myxoid/round cell liposarcoma: FUS::DDIT3 or EWSR1::DDIT3. Dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans: COL1A1::PDGFB. Desmoid fibromatosis: CTNNB1 or APC mutations. Solitary fibrous tumor: NAB2::STAT6 fusion. Alveolar soft part sarcoma: ASPL::TFE3 fusion. Inflammatory myofibroblastic tumor (ALK-rearranged): Various ALK fusions (e.g., TPM3::ALK, CLTC::ALK). The guidelines emphasize that molecular testing should be interpreted in the context of clinical and pathologic features and should not replace expert pathology review. Technical limitations exist, and negative results do not always exclude a suspected diagnosis.

epidemiology.incidence: Soft tissue sarcomas are rare, accounting for approximately 1% of all adult malignancies and 15% of childhood malignancies. In 2022, an estimated 13,190 people will be diagnosed with soft tissue sarcoma (STS) in the United States, with approximately 5,130 deaths. The true incidence is likely underestimated, especially because gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST) were not consistently included in tumor registries before 2001. The most common primary sites are extremities (43%), followed by visceral (19%), retroperitoneum (15%), trunk (10%), and head and neck (9%).
epidemiology.mortality: Approximately 5,130 deaths were estimated for STS in the U.S. in 2022. Mortality rates are higher for high-grade, large, and deeply located tumors, and for certain histologies like retroperitoneal sarcomas.
epidemiology.trends: The incidence of STS has been relatively stable over time. However, improved molecular diagnostics have led to more precise classification, which may affect reported trends for specific subtypes. Survival outcomes have improved with multidisciplinary care, as adherence to NCCN guidelines is associated with improved survival.
epidemiology.demographics: STS can occur at any age, but the incidence increases with age, with a peak in the 6th to 7th decades of life. There is a slight male predominance for most subtypes. The epidemiology can vary by histologic subtype and anatomic location. For example, rhabdomyosarcoma is predominantly a disease of children and adolescents, while liposarcoma and leiomyosarcoma are more common in adults.

risk_factors.detail: Radiation-associated sarcomas are a well-established entity. The risk is cumulative with dose and increases with time since radiation, often developing decades after exposure.
risk_factors.strength: Strong association; a recognized cause of secondary malignancy.

risk_factors.detail:
Germline mutations in several genes confer an increased predisposition to STS. Key syndromes include:
- Li-Fraumeni syndrome (germline TP53 mutations): Associated with increased risk of multiple cancers, including STS (12-21% incidence).
- Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1): Patients are at risk for multiple sarcomas, most notably malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor (MPNST).
- Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) / Gardner syndrome (germline APC mutations): Desmoid tumors occur in 7.5% to 16% of patients with FAP.
- Hereditary retinoblastoma (germline RB1 mutation): Associated with increased risk of STS, particularly leiomyosarcoma.
- Carney-Stratakis syndrome (germline SDH subunit mutations): Predisposes to GIST and paragangliomas.
Exposure to certain chemicals, such as herbicides (e.g., Agent Orange) and vinyl chloride, has been associated with an increased risk of hepatic angiosarcoma and other sarcomas.
risk_factors.strength: Moderate to strong, depending on the specific chemical and exposure level.

risk_factors.detail: Long-standing lymphedema, particularly post-mastectomy, is a risk factor for lymphangiosarcoma (Stewart-Treves syndrome).
Human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8) is causally associated with Kaposi sarcoma, particularly in the setting of immunosuppression (e.g., HIV/AIDS).
risk_factors.strength: Strong, causative association for a specific subtype.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Patients with soft tissue sarcoma of the extremity, body wall, or head/neck typically present with a palpable mass. The mass may be painless or painful, and its location influences symptoms. In the extremities, it may cause swelling, pain, or functional limitation. For superficial trunk or head/neck lesions, the mass may be visible or cause disfigurement. Deep-seated tumors may present with pain due to compression of adjacent structures or nerves. The history should include assessment of tumor growth rate, pain, and any associated neurological symptoms.

symptoms.name: Painless mass
symptoms.detail: A painless, growing mass is the most common presentation.
symptoms.alarm: A rapidly enlarging mass is concerning.

symptoms.name: Pain
symptoms.detail: May be present if the tumor is large, deep, or compresses nerves.
symptoms.alarm: New or increasing pain in a known mass warrants evaluation.

symptoms.name: Functional impairment
symptoms.detail: In extremity tumors, may limit range of motion or strength.

symptoms.name: Neurological symptoms
symptoms.detail: Numbness, tingling, or weakness if nerves are involved.

signs.name: Palpable mass
signs.detail: Firm, fixed or mobile mass on examination.

signs.name: Skin changes
signs.detail: Erythema, ulceration, or visible veins overlying the mass.

signs.name: Lymphadenopathy
signs.detail: Regional lymph nodes may be enlarged, especially in certain subtypes (e.g., angiosarcoma, clear cell sarcoma).

atypical_or_missed: Small, deep-seated tumors may not be palpable initially.
Tumors arising in scar tissue or previously irradiated fields may be mistaken for benign lesions.
Head and neck tumors may mimic other conditions like lymphadenopathy or salivary gland tumors.
Patient delay due to lack of pain or slow growth.

red_flags: Rapidly enlarging mass
Pain or neurological symptoms in a growing mass
Fixed, deep, or large (>5 cm) mass
Mass in a previously irradiated field
Mass associated with fever, weight loss, or night sweats (though rare)
Ulceration or bleeding from the mass
New lymphadenopathy in the setting of a soft tissue mass

diagnostic.rationale: Preferred initial diagnostic procedure to obtain tissue for histologic diagnosis and grading. Should be performed after adequate imaging.
diagnostic.key_findings: Establishes malignancy, specific histologic subtype, and grade (FNCLCC).

diagnostic.rationale: Considered if core needle biopsy is nondiagnostic or inadequate.
diagnostic.key_findings: Same as core needle biopsy.

diagnostic.rationale: Generally discouraged due to limited specimen size; may be acceptable in specialized centers with cytopathology expertise.
diagnostic.key_findings: May suggest sarcoma but often insufficient for definitive subtype and grade.

diagnostic.rationale: Part of ancillary techniques to help subtype tumors (e.g., desmin for muscle differentiation, CD34 for solitary fibrous tumor).
diagnostic.key_findings: Supports morphologic diagnosis and identifies specific tumor types.

diagnostic.rationale: Useful for identifying characteristic genetic aberrations in certain sarcomas (e.g., translocations). NGS may be appropriate for clinical trials or refractory disease.
diagnostic.key_findings: Identifies specific translocations (e.g., SS18-SSX in synovial sarcoma) or mutations.

staging.rationale: Preferred imaging for extremity/body wall/head and neck to define tumor size, depth, and relationship to adjacent structures.
staging.indication: All patients with a suspected sarcoma.

staging.rationale: Preferred for evaluating pulmonary metastases, the most common site of distant spread.
Considered for certain histologies with propensity for abdominal/pelvic metastases (e.g., leiomyosarcoma, myxoid/round cell liposarcoma).
staging.indication: Based on histologic subtype and clinical suspicion.

staging.rationale: May be useful for staging, prognostication, grading, and assessing response to chemotherapy. More common than PET/MRI.
staging.indication: Considered in select situations, e.g., when metastatic disease is suspected or for response assessment.

staging.rationale: Considered for histologies with propensity for bone metastases (e.g., alveolar soft part sarcoma, clear cell sarcoma).
Considered for histologies with propensity for CNS metastases (e.g., alveolar soft part sarcoma, angiosarcoma).

biomarkers.rationale: Grade is a key prognostic factor and guides therapy.
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis from biopsy or resection specimen.
biomarkers.mandatory: Yes, if applicable to the histologic subtype.

biomarkers.rationale: Can confirm diagnosis and may have prognostic implications (e.g., PAX3-FOXO1 vs. PAX7-FOXO1 in alveolar RMS).
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis when subtype is unclear or for specific histologies.
biomarkers.mandatory: Not always mandatory but recommended for certain subtypes.

biomarkers.rationale: May predict response to immunotherapy (e.g., pembrolizumab) in select cases.
biomarkers.when: Considered in advanced/metastatic disease for potential clinical trials or immunotherapy.

do_not_miss: Biopsy after adequate imaging (do not biopsy before imaging).
Multidisciplinary team evaluation before treatment.
Assessment for genetic cancer syndromes (e.g., Li-Fraumeni, neurofibromatosis) in appropriate patients.
Evaluation for distress.
avoid: Unplanned excision without prior imaging or biopsy.
FNA as the sole diagnostic procedure in most cases (due to limited tissue).
Over-reliance on CT for local staging in extremity tumors (MRI preferred).
Routine bone scan for all patients (indicated only for specific histologies).

staging.system: AJCC 8th Edition for soft tissue sarcoma of the extremity, superficial trunk, and head/neck.

t_stages.description: Tumor >5 cm and ≤10 cm in greatest dimension.
Tumor >10 cm and ≤15 cm in greatest dimension.

n_stages.description: No regional lymph node metastasis or unknown lymph node status.

stage_groups.criteria: T1, N0, M0, G1 or GX
T2-4, N0, M0, G1 or GX

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Small high-grade tumor, increased risk of metastasis.
Large high-grade tumor, high risk of metastasis.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative with multimodality therapy; may require neoadjuvant therapy.

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, N1, M0, any G; or any T, any N, M1, any G
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative; systemic therapy and local control as appropriate.

staging_pearls: Lymph node metastasis is rare in most extremity sarcomas but more common in certain histologies (e.g., angiosarcoma, clear cell sarcoma, alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma).
Grade is a critical prognostic factor; low-grade tumors have a much lower risk of metastasis.
Tumor size is a continuous variable; larger tumors (>5 cm) have higher risk.
AJCC 8th edition uses size cutoffs of 5, 10, and 15 cm for T stages.
For head and neck sarcomas, T4 is defined by invasion of specific structures (orbit, skull base, brain, etc.) rather than size alone.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcomas often present late with vague symptoms due to their deep location. Patients may report abdominal fullness, early satiety, or a palpable abdominal mass. Pain may be present if the tumor compresses adjacent organs or nerves. Weight loss and fatigue can occur with advanced disease. A history of abdominal surgery or trauma may be present in some cases.

symptoms.name: Abdominal mass or fullness
symptoms.detail: Often large and discovered incidentally or on imaging.

symptoms.name: Pain
symptoms.detail: May be dull and constant, or colicky if bowel obstruction occurs.

symptoms.name: Early satiety or nausea
symptoms.detail: Due to compression of stomach or intestines.

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Unintentional weight loss may occur with advanced disease.

symptoms.name: Abdominal distension
symptoms.detail: May be due to mass effect or ascites.

signs.name: Palpable abdominal mass
signs.detail: Often large and may be fixed.

signs.name: Ascites
signs.detail: May be present in advanced disease.

signs.name: Hepatomegaly
signs.detail: If liver is involved or compressed.

atypical_or_missed: Many tumors are asymptomatic and discovered incidentally on imaging.
Symptoms may be attributed to more common conditions like irritable bowel syndrome.
Tumors arising in the pelvis may cause urinary or pelvic symptoms.
Gastrointestinal bleeding may occur if the tumor invades the bowel.

red_flags: Rapidly enlarging abdominal mass
Unexplained weight loss
Abdominal pain with fever or signs of infection
Bowel obstruction or urinary symptoms
Ascites
History of prior radiation to the abdomen

diagnostic.rationale: Preferred imaging modality for evaluation of retroperitoneal masses, providing details on size, location, and relationship to adjacent structures.
diagnostic.key_findings: Defines tumor extent, invasion into adjacent organs, and possible lymphadenopathy.

diagnostic.rationale: Useful in certain circumstances, e.g., to evaluate relationship to vessels or if CT contrast is contraindicated.
diagnostic.key_findings: May better delineate tumor margins and vascular involvement.

diagnostic.rationale: Preferred over surgical biopsy; should avoid the free intra-abdominal space to prevent seeding. Indicated if neoadjuvant therapy is being considered or if diagnosis is uncertain.
diagnostic.key_findings: Establishes histologic subtype and grade.

diagnostic.rationale: For staging to evaluate for pulmonary metastases.
diagnostic.indication: All patients with histologies that metastasize to lungs.

staging.rationale: Primary imaging for local staging and follow-up.
For distant staging.
staging.indication: All patients, except possibly for well-differentiated liposarcoma without dedifferentiation.

staging.rationale: May help differentiate between well-differentiated and dedifferentiated liposarcoma and guide biopsy site.

biomarkers.when: At diagnosis from biopsy or resection.

biomarkers.rationale: May confirm diagnosis (e.g., MDM2 amplification in well-differentiated/dedifferentiated liposarcoma).
biomarkers.mandatory: Not always mandatory but recommended for specific histologies.

do_not_miss: Avoid biopsy that contaminates the peritoneal cavity.
Multidisciplinary team evaluation before treatment.
Consider genetic syndromes (e.g., Li-Fraumeni, HNPCC) in appropriate patients.
avoid: Unplanned surgical exploration without prior imaging and biopsy.
Surgical biopsy through the peritoneal cavity if possible.
Routine chest X-ray for staging (CT chest preferred).

staging.system: AJCC 8th Edition for soft tissue sarcoma of the retroperitoneum.

t_stages.description: Tumor >5 cm and ≤10 cm in greatest dimension.
Tumor >10 cm and ≤15 cm in greatest dimension.

n_stages.description: No regional lymph node metastasis or unknown lymph node status.

stage_groups.criteria: T1, N0, M0, G1 or GX
T2-4, N0, M0, G1 or GX
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative with surgery; consider adjuvant therapy based on margin status.
Curative with surgery; consider adjuvant therapy.
Curative with surgery; consider neoadjuvant/adjuvant therapy.
Curative with surgery; consider neoadjuvant/adjuvant therapy.

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, N1, M0, any G; or any T, any N, M1, any G
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative; systemic therapy and local control as appropriate.

staging_pearls: Retroperitoneal sarcomas are often large at presentation due to delayed symptoms.
Complete surgical resection with negative margins is the most important prognostic factor.
Lymph node metastasis is uncommon; if present, regional lymph node dissection is recommended.
Well-differentiated liposarcoma has a very low risk of metastasis but can recur locally.
Dedifferentiated liposarcoma behaves more aggressively.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Desmoid tumors are locally invasive but nonmetastasizing fibroblastic neoplasms. They can arise in any anatomic location, including the abdominal wall (especially in young women, often during or after pregnancy), intra-abdominal (mesentery, retroperitoneum), and extremities or trunk (extra-abdominal). Symptoms depend on location: abdominal wall desmoids present as a palpable, often painful mass; intra-abdominal desmoids may cause bowel obstruction, abdominal pain, or a palpable mass; extremity/trunk desmoids present as a deep, slow-growing mass that may be painful or cause functional impairment.

symptoms.name: Palpable mass
symptoms.detail: Slow-growing, may be painful.

symptoms.name: Pain
symptoms.detail: May be due to mass effect or nerve compression.

symptoms.name: Bowel obstruction (intra-abdominal)
symptoms.detail: Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain.

symptoms.name: Functional impairment (extremity)
symptoms.detail: Limited range of motion, weakness.

atypical_or_missed: May mimic other tumors (e.g., sarcoma, carcinoma) on imaging.
Intra-abdominal desmoids may not be palpable on physical exam.
Spontaneous regression can occur, especially in newly diagnosed tumors.
Patients with familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) are at higher risk.

red_flags: Rapidly growing mass
New or worsening pain
Bowel obstruction symptoms (intra-abdominal desmoids)
History of FAP or Gardner syndrome
Previous abdominal surgery (risk factor for intra-abdominal desmoids)

diagnostic.rationale: To confirm diagnosis and rule out other tumors. May be omitted if complete resection is planned and imaging is characteristic.
diagnostic.key_findings: Bland spindle cells in a collagenous stroma, infiltrative borders, no significant atypia or mitotic activity.

diagnostic.rationale: Preferred imaging for local extent and follow-up. For abdominal wall, ultrasound may also be used.
diagnostic.key_findings: Low signal intensity on T1 and T2 weighted images, variable enhancement.

diagnostic.rationale: For intra-abdominal desmoids to evaluate extent and relationship to bowel.
diagnostic.key_findings: Soft tissue mass, may cause bowel displacement or obstruction.

staging.rationale: To define local extent. There is no formal staging system for desmoids as they are nonmetastatic.

biomarkers.rationale: Nuclear staining supports diagnosis of desmoid tumor.
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis when diagnosis is uncertain.

biomarkers.rationale: May help confirm diagnosis and has potential prognostic implications (mutation 45F associated with higher recurrence risk).
biomarkers.when: Considered in select cases, especially for research or risk stratification.
biomarkers.mandatory: Not mandatory for routine clinical care.

do_not_miss: Evaluation for Gardner syndrome/FAP in patients with desmoid tumors.
Consider multidisciplinary team for management decisions.
Imaging before biopsy to plan approach.
avoid: Aggressive surgery for intra-abdominal desmoids if it would cause significant morbidity.
Assuming all desmoids require immediate treatment; observation is appropriate for many.

staging.system: No formal staging system for desmoid tumors as they are nonmetastasizing. Risk stratification is based on location, size, symptoms, and growth pattern.
staging_pearls: Desmoids are not staged in the AJCC system.
Management is based on anatomic location (abdominal wall, intra-abdominal, extra-abdominal) and symptoms.
Spontaneous regression can occur in up to 20% of patients, supporting an initial period of observation in asymptomatic patients.
Intra-abdominal desmoids are more challenging to treat and may be associated with FAP.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS) is the most common soft tissue sarcoma in children and adolescents but is rare in adults. In adults, pleomorphic RMS is more common. Presentation depends on anatomic site: head and neck tumors may present with proptosis, nasal obstruction, or ear drainage; genitourinary tumors may cause hematuria or obstruction; extremity tumors present as a rapidly growing mass. Adults tend to have higher-grade histologies and poorer prognosis compared to children.

symptoms.name: Rapidly growing mass
symptoms.detail: Often painless, but may be painful if large or compressing structures.
symptoms.alarm: Rapid growth in a child or young adult.

symptoms.name: Pain
symptoms.detail: May occur with nerve involvement or large tumor size.
symptoms.alarm: New pain in a known mass.

symptoms.name: Functional impairment
symptoms.detail: If near a joint or in the orbit, may limit movement.

symptoms.name: Systemic symptoms
symptoms.detail: Fever, weight loss may occur in advanced disease.

signs.name: Lymphadenopathy
signs.detail: Regional nodes may be involved, especially in alveolar RMS.

atypical_or_missed: In adults, may be mistaken for other high-grade sarcomas.
Head and neck tumors may mimic inflammatory conditions.
Genitourinary tumors may be attributed to benign conditions initially.
Metastatic disease at presentation is more common in adults.

red_flags: Rapidly enlarging mass in a child or young adult
Proptosis or cranial nerve palsy in head/neck mass
Hematuria or urinary obstruction in a pelvic mass
Lymphadenopathy associated with a soft tissue mass

diagnostic.rationale: Required for diagnosis and subtype classification (embryonal, alveolar, pleomorphic).
diagnostic.key_findings: Small round blue cells (embryonal/alveolar) or pleomorphic spindle cells (pleomorphic RMS). IHC for desmin, myogenin, MyoD1 positive.

diagnostic.rationale: To identify characteristic translocations in alveolar RMS (PAX3-FOXO1, PAX7-FOXO1) which have prognostic significance.
diagnostic.key_findings: Fusion gene detection.

staging.rationale: To define local extent.
To evaluate for pulmonary metastases.
For abdominal/pelvic primary tumors or to evaluate lymph nodes.
May be useful for initial staging due to possibility of nodal metastases and unusual sites of disease in adults.
staging.indication: Considered in adults and for staging alveolar RMS.

staging.rationale: For evaluation of bone marrow involvement, especially in alveolar RMS.
staging.indication: Indicated for alveolar RMS or metastatic workup.

biomarkers.rationale: Important for prognosis and treatment stratification.

do_not_miss: Referral to institution with expertise in RMS.
Multidisciplinary team including pediatric, medical, surgical, and radiation oncologists.
Staging workup including bone marrow for alveolar RMS.
Genetic counseling for patients with embryonal RMS (risk of Li-Fraumeni).
avoid: Treating pleomorphic RMS like other adult sarcomas without considering RMS-specific regimens.
Omitting nodal evaluation in alveolar RMS.
FNA as the sole diagnostic procedure (insufficient for subtyping).

staging.system: AJCC 8th Edition for soft tissue sarcoma (same as extremity/trunk/head and neck) with additional risk stratification based on histology, site, size, and molecular features.

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, N0, M0, nonalveolar histology (embryonal), favorable site (orbit, head/neck nonparameningeal, genitourinary nonbladder/prostate)
Any T, N0, M0, nonalveolar histology, unfavorable site; or any T, N0, M0, alveolar histology, favorable site
Any T, N1, M0, any histology; or any T, N0, M0, alveolar histology, unfavorable site

stage_groups.treatment_intent: Systemic therapy with local control as appropriate.

staging_pearls: RMS uses a different staging and risk stratification than other STS, incorporating histologic subtype, site, and molecular features.
Alveolar histology is associated with worse prognosis than embryonal.
Favorable sites (orbit, nonparameningeal head/neck, genitourinary nonbladder/prostate) have better outcomes.
Adults with RMS have worse prognosis than children and often present with pleomorphic histology.
Nodal involvement is more common in alveolar RMS and should be evaluated.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Atypical lipomatous tumor (ALT) and well-differentiated liposarcoma (WDLPS) are the same entity, with ALT used for superficial lesions (extremity, superficial trunk) and WDLPS for deep-seated lesions (retroperitoneum, mediastinum). They present as slow-growing, painless masses. Extremity ALTs are often large (>10 cm) and deep-seated within muscle. Retroperitoneal WDLPS may grow to a very large size before causing symptoms like abdominal fullness or pain. These tumors have very low metastatic potential but can recur locally if not completely resected.

symptoms.name: Painless mass
symptoms.detail: Slow-growing, may be present for years.
symptoms.alarm: Rapid growth or development of a new solid component (suggests dedifferentiation).

symptoms.name: Pain or pressure symptoms
symptoms.detail: If large or compressing structures.
symptoms.alarm: New pain in a long-standing mass.

symptoms.name: Functional limitation (extremity)
symptoms.detail: If near a joint.

signs.name: Soft, rubbery mass
signs.detail: May feel lipoma-like but larger and deeper.

signs.name: Firm or hard areas within mass

atypical_or_missed: May be misdiagnosed as a lipoma if not imaged properly.
Retroperitoneal tumors may not be palpable until very large.
Dedifferentiation can occur, leading to more aggressive behavior.

red_flags: Large (>10 cm) deep-seated fatty mass
Mass with solid components on imaging
Rapid growth in a previously stable fatty mass
History of prior radiation to the area

diagnostic.rationale: Preferred imaging to characterize the fatty lesion and identify any solid components suggesting dedifferentiation.
diagnostic.key_findings: Fatty signal with internal septations and possible nodular enhancement.

diagnostic.rationale: Indicated if imaging is not classic or to evaluate solid components. May be omitted for typical extremity ALT if complete resection is planned.
diagnostic.key_findings: Adipocytes with atypia and scattered hyperchromatic nuclei, lipoblasts.

diagnostic.rationale: Confirmatory for WDLPS/dedifferentiated liposarcoma.
diagnostic.key_findings: Amplification of 12q13-15 region including MDM2 gene.

staging.rationale: For local staging.
Not routinely needed for extremity ALT due to very low metastatic risk, but may be considered for retroperitoneal WDLPS.
staging.indication: Not mandatory for extremity ALT; consider for retroperitoneal WDLPS.

biomarkers.when: At diagnosis if histology is uncertain.

biomarkers.rationale: Often co-amplified with MDM2, can be targeted with CDK4 inhibitors in dedifferentiated liposarcoma.

do_not_miss: Evaluation for dedifferentiation (solid components on imaging).
Consideration for limb-sparing surgery rather than amputation.
Referral to sarcoma center for management.
avoid: Routine chest imaging for extremity ALT (low metastatic risk).
Aggressive surgery if margins can be achieved with less morbidity.

staging.system: AJCC 8th Edition for soft tissue sarcoma (same as extremity/trunk).

stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative with surgery; consider adjuvant therapy if dedifferentiated.

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, N1, M0, any G; or any T, any N, M1, any G
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Metastatic disease (very rare for WDLPS unless dedifferentiated).

staging_pearls: ALT/WDLPS are low-grade tumors with very low metastatic potential (<5%).
Local recurrence is the main issue, especially for retroperitoneal tumors.
Dedifferentiated liposarcoma behaves more aggressively and is staged as high-grade.
MDM2 amplification is characteristic and can be used for diagnosis.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Borderline phyllodes tumor of the breast is a fibroepithelial neoplasm with a borderline risk of recurrence. It typically presents as a rapidly growing, palpable breast mass, often in women aged 30-50 years. The mass may be painless or tender. It can be mistaken for fibroadenoma clinically and radiologically. There is a risk of local recurrence after surgical excision, but metastasis is rare.

symptoms.name: Palpable breast mass
symptoms.detail: Often large (>3 cm), rapidly growing.
symptoms.alarm: Rapid growth or change in size.

symptoms.name: Pain or tenderness
symptoms.detail: May be present.
symptoms.alarm: New pain in a breast mass.

symptoms.name: Skin changes
symptoms.detail: Rarely, ulceration or discoloration.

atypical_or_missed: May be misdiagnosed as fibroadenoma on core biopsy.
Small tumors may be indistinguishable from fibroadenoma.
Phyllodes tumors can have areas of necrosis or hemorrhage.

red_flags: Rapidly growing breast mass
Large breast mass (>3 cm)
Mass in a woman older than 35 years
Core biopsy showing cellular fibroepithelial lesion

diagnostic.rationale: Initial diagnostic procedure, but may not distinguish between fibroadenoma and phyllodes tumor due to sampling variability.
diagnostic.key_findings: Cellular stroma with leaf-like architecture, increased mitotic activity, borderline features.

diagnostic.test: Breast MRI with and without contrast
diagnostic.rationale: Helps define extent of tumor and relationship to surrounding tissue.
diagnostic.key_findings: Enhancing mass, may show internal septations.

diagnostic.rationale: Definitive diagnosis and treatment. Wide excision with ≥1 cm margin is recommended.
diagnostic.key_findings: Histologic confirmation of borderline phyllodes tumor.

staging.rationale: For local evaluation.
Not routinely needed for borderline phyllodes due to low metastatic risk, but may be considered for malignant phyllodes.
staging.indication: Consider for malignant phyllodes; not standard for borderline.

biomarkers.test: Histologic features (mitotic count, borders, stromal overgrowth)
biomarkers.rationale: Used to classify as benign, borderline, or malignant.

do_not_miss: Genetic counseling and testing for breast and sarcoma genes (e.g., BRCA, TP53) as recommended.
Sufficient margin at initial excision (≥1 cm).
Multidisciplinary team evaluation.
avoid: Mastectomy for borderline phyllodes if breast conservation with adequate margins is possible.
Axillary staging (lymph node involvement is rare).

staging.system: No formal AJCC staging for phyllodes tumors; they are classified as benign, borderline, or malignant based on histology.
staging_pearls: Borderline phyllodes tumors have a risk of local recurrence but very low metastatic potential.
Wide local excision with ≥1 cm margins is the standard treatment.
If margins are positive, re-excision is recommended.
Adjuvant radiation may be considered for increased risk of local recurrence (e.g., mitoses >30/10 hpf, size >10 cm, malignant heterologous elements).

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Malignant phyllodes tumor of the breast is a rare fibroepithelial neoplasm with high-grade stromal component and potential for metastasis. It typically presents as a large, rapidly growing breast mass, often in women aged 40-50 years. The mass may be painful or tender, and skin changes or ulceration may occur. Metastasis can occur hematogenously to lungs, bone, and liver.

symptoms.name: Rapidly growing breast mass
symptoms.detail: Often large (>5 cm), may be painful.

symptoms.name: Pain or tenderness
symptoms.detail: Common due to large size.

symptoms.name: Skin changes
symptoms.detail: Redness, ulceration, or necrosis.

symptoms.name: Systemic symptoms
symptoms.detail: Weight loss, fatigue with metastatic disease.

signs.name: Large, firm mass
signs.detail: Often occupies much of the breast.

signs.name: Axillary lymphadenopathy
signs.detail: Rare; if present, may be reactive or metastatic.

atypical_or_missed: May be misdiagnosed as fibroadenoma or carcinoma.
Core biopsy may underestimate grade.
Metastatic disease may be asymptomatic initially.

red_flags: Large (>5 cm), rapidly growing breast mass
Mass with skin changes or ulceration
Mass in a woman older than 40 years
Core biopsy showing high-grade stromal component

diagnostic.rationale: Initial diagnosis, but may not capture highest grade areas.
diagnostic.key_findings: Markedly increased stromal cellularity, atypia, mitotic figures, overgrowth, and malignant heterologous elements.

diagnostic.test: Breast MRI with and without contrast
diagnostic.rationale: To define extent of tumor and evaluate for multifocality.
diagnostic.key_findings: Large, enhancing mass with possible necrosis.

diagnostic.rationale: For staging due to risk of metastasis to lungs, bone, liver.
May be used for staging to evaluate for metastases.
If bone pain or elevated alkaline phosphatase.

staging.rationale: Standard for staging due to metastatic potential.
Considered for brain metastases, especially if neurological symptoms.
staging.indication: If symptoms or high clinical suspicion.

biomarkers.test: Mitotic count, stromal overgrowth, heterologous elements
biomarkers.rationale: Features that define malignancy and prognosis.

do_not_miss: Wide surgical excision with ≥1 cm margins (mastectomy may be needed).
No axillary staging unless lymph nodes are clinically suspicious.
Multidisciplinary team including breast surgeon, medical oncologist, radiation oncologist.
Genetic counseling and testing.
avoid: Breast conservation surgery if adequate margins cannot be achieved.
Delay in surgery due to growth rate.

staging.system: No formal AJCC staging for phyllodes tumors; classified as malignant based on histology. Metastatic workup is similar to other breast sarcomas.
staging_pearls: Malignant phyllodes tumors have a significant risk of metastasis (up to 20-30%).
Metastases are hematogenous, most commonly to lungs.
Axillary lymph node involvement is rare; if present, may indicate carcinoma or high-grade sarcoma.
Wide local excision with ≥1 cm margins is standard; mastectomy may be required.
Adjuvant radiation is recommended for increased risk of local recurrence.
Systemic therapy is considered for high-risk features or metastatic disease.
